staff_draft:
I trace the shape of what I want to study back to the abacus class my parents enrolled me in at five. By the time I was ten I had finished every level of the UCMAS Mental Arithmetic programme, and what stayed with me was less the speed than the habit. There is a way to look at a number and have it click, and that habit is the through-line of everything I have done since.

My Class X aggregate of 98 percent on the ICSE board carried four perfect 100s, in Mathematics, Biology, History and Civics, and Literature in English. The last one is the part of my own record that surprised me, because it pushed back on the idea that I am narrowly a maths person. In Class XI, my Mathematics aggregate of 91.5 and Physics aggregate of 91.15 were the two strongest scores on a five-subject report card, and they continue to be the two subjects I most want to keep at the core of an undergraduate programme.

The preparation that does not show up on a transcript started earlier. In Class IX I completed the MENTORx Foundation Course on Entrepreneurship and Innovation and was singled out as "exceptional performance" on the certificate. The half I returned to afterwards was problem identification, the difference between an idea you like and a problem somebody actually has. In 2020 I went through the WhiteHat Jr Game Developer cohort and my certificate flagged UI and UX work specifically. At eleven I did not know that was a discipline, but I was already drawn to making interfaces somebody else could pick up. In Class XII I represented Guru Nanak at the BCM Techno Fest inter-school quiz, which is the more obvious academic activity, but the two earlier ones are the ones I keep returning to.

What I want to build, as an undergraduate and after, is software products where I am the person doing problem identification and then deciding what to build. The MENTORx course put that framing in front of me at fourteen. Five years on, I would like to study the foundations under it.

I am pursuing an undergraduate degree in engineering with a computing or product orientation. Mathematics and Physics are the parts of my Class XII timetable that have stayed strongest, and a programme that keeps those two in the core while giving me design and prototyping space is the shape that fits the work I have already started. Modules on systems thinking, software design, and human-computer interaction are the parts of a programme structure I would most want to engage with.

The through-line is an early quantitative foundation, a Class IX moment that taught me solving for a problem is different from solving for a mark, and four years of school work that stayed strong while the interests outside the textbook kept compounding. I am ready for the part of the path where the academic and the practical sides finally sit in the same programme.

recipient_role: Principal, Guru Nanak International Public School, Ludhiana
reason_brief: Principal of student's senior secondary school across Class XI and XII; co-signs every progress remark on his record.
staff_draft:
To Whom It May Concern,
I am proud to write this letter of recommendation for Pratham Aggarwal, a student at our school, Guru Nanak International Public School.
Pratham is an exceptionally bright and dedicated student who has consistently excelled in subjects such as Mathematics, Chemistry, and Physics. He possesses outstanding logical and analytical abilities, which are clearly reflected in his academic performance. Whether in Mathematics, Physics, or Chemistry, he has consistently been among the top-ranking students in the school.
Apart from his academic excellence, Pratham is an active participant in classroom discussions and group activities. He represented the school in the Techno Fest Quiz Competition and made us proud through his stellar performance and confident representation of the institution.
As a member of the Student Council, Pratham served as the Head of the Event Organising Team, where he demonstrated exceptional leadership and organisational skills. He ensured that every responsibility entrusted to him and his team was executed seamlessly and efficiently.
Pratham has also delivered outstanding performances in debating and declamation competitions, reflecting his excellent command over language, confidence in public speaking, and awareness of current affairs and the environment around him.
What truly sets Pratham apart is his eagerness to learn beyond the classroom. In addition to school academics, he has independently pursued coding and programming through external learning resources. He has also participated in entrepreneurship workshops, where he impressed everyone with his innovative ideas and remarkable performance.
Pratham strongly believes in all-round development. He possesses excellent leadership qualities, teamwork skills, confidence, and strong academic knowledge. Along with his achievements, he is widely appreciated for his humble and respectful behaviour among his peers and teachers alike.
I am confident that Pratham Aggarwal will continue to excel in all his future academic and professional pursuits and will be a valuable addition to any institution he becomes a part of.
I wholeheartedly recommend him without any reservation. You may contact me on my email ……………………..for further information  .
Sincerely,
[Name]
Principal 
Guru Nanak International Public School

recipient_role: Mathematics, Class XII Non-Medical, Guru Nanak International Public School, Ludhiana
reason_brief: Mathematics teacher across Class XI and Class XII; observed Pratham's proof-discipline through limits, continuity, and differentiability units.
staff_draft:
Date: [TODAY]

To Whom It May Concern,

I have taught Pratham Aggarwal Mathematics in our Class XI and Class XII Non-Medical section at Guru Nanak International Public School, Ludhiana, across the 2024-25 and 2025-26 academic sessions. My Class XII Mathematics group this year runs at 38 students. Across the Class XI and XII cycles I run, the median student earns somewhere between 72 and 78 percent at the annual aggregate. Pratham earned 91.5 on the Class XI annual Mathematics paper, in a year when the section as a whole came in below my usual mean. The number I want your committee to read off that figure is not the absolute mark but the gap from the median.

Pratham is a student who treats Mathematics as a discipline rather than a scoring instrument. In the first month of Class XI I had the section work through the limits-and-continuity unit. Most students at that stage will accept an answer that pattern-matches the worked example in the textbook. Pratham would not. He spent the better part of two consecutive classes trying to construct a counterexample to a continuity claim I had set as a quick check, until I had to stop him and concede that the claim was an oversimplified version of the actual theorem. He had found the gap. He had also, in the process, lost the first set of marks of the year on a quick-test on the same unit, because he had been thinking about a question I had not asked rather than the one I had. The next two units I taught he was visibly more cautious about that trade-off, and would slow down mid-class to check whether a side-direction he had spotted was worth following or not. By the second term he had relearned how to hold both threads, the immediate problem in front of him and the next layer behind it. We talked about the early miss afterwards. He took the lesson without defensiveness and adjusted.

What I look for in a Mathematics student, at this stage, is a particular kind of patience: the willingness to sit with an unsolved problem rather than reach for a method. Pratham has that patience. In the second-term unit on continuity and differentiability he produced a proof for one of the harder textbook problems that did not use the worked-example approach the rest of the section was relying on. His version was longer but more general. I marked it full marks and used it as a board demonstration for the cohort. He was visibly uncomfortable with that, which is the second qualifier I would write about him to a committee. Pratham does not enjoy being shown to the room, even when his work warrants it. The second-term grand total he carried into Class XII reflects that personality more than it reflects his Mathematics. He is, by my reading of him, a stronger student than his classroom rank suggests, because he uses his Mathematics time for substance rather than for visibility.

For an applicant whose stated direction is engineering or computing, the Mathematics teacher's view that matters is whether the student treats the subject as a foundation or as a hurdle. Pratham treats it as a foundation. I recommend him to your programme on the basis of two years' classroom observation and on the strength of work-product that has, repeatedly, surprised me on the upside. Should your panel wish to confirm the observations in this letter, my office at the school is the best route.

Sincerely,

[Class XII Mathematics Teacher]
Mathematics, Class XII Non-Medical, Guru Nanak International Public School, Ludhiana

recipient_role: Physics, Class XII Non-Medical, Guru Nanak International Public School, Ludhiana
reason_brief: Class XI and XII Physics teacher; supervised practicals where his rotational-dynamics and optics work earned 29 of 30.
staff_draft:
Date: [TODAY]

To Whom It May Concern,

Across the Class XI and Class XII Physics groups I have taught at Guru Nanak International Public School, Ludhiana, a Physics-practicals score above 28 out of 30 marks a student who is good in the laboratory and not only on the theory paper. Pratham Aggarwal earned 29 of 30 on the Class XI Physics practicals, in both halves of the year. That figure, paired with a theory band that has held in the high 80s and low 90s, is the cleanest empirical signal I can offer your committee on a quant-track applicant. Pratham has been in my Non-Medical Physics section across the 2024-25 and 2025-26 sessions.

The classroom work is where the theory paper lives. The laboratory is where I learn what a student is actually like with Physics, and I would rather use this letter on the laboratory than on the paper. Two specific incidents from Pratham's Class XI year are worth recording.

The first is from the rotational-dynamics unit late in the first term. The practical called for the student to determine the moment of inertia of a flywheel about its central axis from a falling-mass setup. Pratham's pair came back with a result that disagreed with the theoretical value the textbook supplied by a margin large enough that I would normally have called for a redo. Pratham did not ask for a redo. He sat back at the bench, recomputed the height of the drop with a steel rule that he had not used the first time, and identified that the original measurement had been taken from the bottom of the falling mass rather than from its centre of gravity. The corrected value came in within the expected error band on the second attempt. He had not been told that the centre of gravity was the right anchor. He had reasoned to it from the setup. That habit, the willingness to suspect his own measurement before he suspects the apparatus, is the trait I most want a Physics committee to know about him.

The second incident is from the optics block in the second term. The class was running the focal-length-of-a-convex-mirror experiment, which is one of the practicals where students routinely report figures that are physically impossible because they have not separated image and object distances correctly. Pratham reached the end of the practical with a value that was too clean. His residuals were unphysically small. When I asked him to defend the result he agreed, without prompting, that the residuals looked suspect, and he asked whether the issue might be that he had taken his readings on a parallax that was not in fact eliminated. We checked together. He had eliminated parallax correctly. The data was, on inspection, real. He had simply got it right the first time. The qualifier in the story is not in the data but in his readiness to assume his own number was wrong before he assumed the apparatus had been kind. That is a Physics temperament, and it is rare at sixteen.

Outside the laboratory, Pratham handles the algebra of Physics with the same care. He reaches for units before he reaches for figures. He sanity-checks the order of magnitude of an answer before he believes it. He treats the textbook's worked examples as cases of a method rather than as templates to be re-applied. For a quant-track applicant moving into engineering or computing, where the cost of an undetected sign error or an unphysical answer compounds, this temperament is the one I would weight most heavily.

I recommend Pratham Aggarwal to your programme firmly, on the strength of two years of classroom and laboratory observation. I have taught Physics at this school long enough to know the difference between a student who is good at the subject and a student who has the right relationship with it. He is the second kind. I am available at the school if your committee would value any direct conversation, and I would be glad to add detail on either of the two laboratory incidents above.

Sincerely,

[Class XII Physics Teacher]
Physics, Class XII Non-Medical, Guru Nanak International Public School, Ludhiana
